# pathlib3x

Version v2.0.3 as of 2023-07-20 see [Changelog]

{{ build_badge }} {{ codeql }} {{ license }} {{ pypi }}
{{ pypi-downloads }} {{ black }} {{ codecov }} {{ cc_maintain }} {{ cc_issues }} {{ cc_coverage }} {{ snyk }}

% for the pypi status link note the dashes, not the underscore !

Backport of Python 3.11.0a0 pathlib for Python 3.6, 3.7, 3.8, 3.9, 3.10 with a few tweaks to make it compatible.

added wrappers to shutil copy, copy2, rmtree, copytree and other useful functions.

fully typed PEP561 package

this will be updated periodically to have the latest version of pathlib available on 3.6, 3.7, 3.8, 3.9, 3.10 and probably others.

# WHY pathlib3x ?

There are a number of small, but very handy features added in pathlib over the last python versions,
so I want to be able to use those also on older python versions.

if You are used to :

```
import pathlib

pathlib.Path('some_file').unlink(missing_ok=True)
```

You will have no luck on python 3.7 - because the "missing_ok" parameter was added in python3.8

Of course You can do :

```
import pathlib

try:
    pathlib.Path('some_file').unlink()
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass
```

but that clutters the code unnecessarily. So just use :

```
import pathlib3x as pathlib
```

and You can enjoy the latest pathlib features even on older python versions.

Some own extensions to that pathlib will be added probably over time. At the moment we added some wrappers to shutil like "copy", "rmtree", "copytree", so
You can do :

```
import pathlib3x as pathlib
my_file = pathlib.Path('/etc/hosts')
to_file = pathlib.Path('/tmp/foo')
my_file.copy(to_file)
```

If You have some nice features for pathlib, let me know - I will consider to integrate them.

______________________________________________________________________

automated tests, Github Actions, Documentation, Badges, etc. are managed with [PizzaCutter](https://github.com/bitranox/PizzaCutter) (cookiecutter on steroids)

Python version required: 3.8.0 or newer

tested on recent linux with python 3.8, 3.9, 3.10, 3.11, 3.12-dev, pypy-3.9, pypy-3.10 - architectures: amd64

[100% (for my added functions) code coverage](https://codeclimate.com/github/bitranox/pathlib3x/test_coverage), flake8 style checking ,mypy static type checking ,tested under [Linux, macOS, Windows](https://github.com/bitranox/pathlib3x/actions/workflows/python-package.yml), automatic daily builds and monitoring

## Usage

just check out the latest python documentation :  <https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html> and select 3.10 Branch

Additional Features are documented here :

PurePath.append_suffix(suffix)

: Return a new path with the *suffix* appended. If the original path doesn’t have a suffix, the new suffix is appended.
  If the original path have a suffix, the new suffix will be appended at the end.
  If *suffix* is an empty string the original Path will be returned.

```python
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> p.append_suffix('.bz2')
PureWindowsPath('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz.bz2')
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('README')
>>> p.append_suffix('.txt')
PureWindowsPath('README.txt')
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('README.txt')
>>> p.append_suffix('')
PureWindowsPath('README.txt')
```

PurePath.is_path_instance(\_\_obj)

: Return True if *\_\_obj* is instance of the original pathlib.Path or pathlib3x.Path.
  Useful if You need to check for Path type, in an environment were You mix pathlib and pathlib3x

```python
>>> import pathlib3x
>>> import pathlib

>>> pathlib3x_path = pathlib3x.Path('some_path')  # this might happen in another module !
>>> pathlib_path = pathlib.Path('some_path')
>>> isinstance(pathlib3x_path, pathlib.Path)
False
>>> isinstance(pathlib_path, pathlib3x.Path)
False

# in such cases were You need to mix pathlib and pathlib3x in different modules, use:
>>> pathlib3x_path.Path.is_path_instance(pathlib3x_path)
True
>>> pathlib3x_path.Path.is_path_instance(pathlib_path)
True
```

PurePath.replace_parts(old, new, count=-1)

: Return a new Path with parts replaced. If the Original Path or *old* has no parts, the Original Path will be returned.
  On Windows, the replacement operation is not case sensitive, because of case folding on drives, directory and filenames.
  You can also replace absolute paths with relative paths what is quite handy - just be aware that the results might
  look unexpected, especially on Windows.

  *old, new* can be pathlib.Path or Path-like objects

  if the Original Path is resolved, You should probably also resolve *old* and *new* - because if symlinks are involved,
  the results might be unexpected.

  be aware of case folding in windows, the file "c:/Test/test.txt" is the same as "c:/test/Test.TXT"

```python
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> p.replace_parts(PureWindowsPath('C:/downloads'), PureWindowsPath('D:/uploads'))
PureWindowsPath('D:/uploads/pathlib.tar.gz')

>>> p = PureWindowsPath('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> p.replace_parts('C:/downloads','D:/uploads')
PureWindowsPath('D:/uploads/pathlib.tar.gz')

# handy to replace source directories with target directories on copy or move operations :
>>> source_dir = pathlib.Path('c:/source_dir')
>>> target_dir = pathlib.Path('c:/target_dir')
>>> source_files = source_dir.glob('**/*.txt')
>>> for source in source_files:
        target = source.replace_parts(source_dir, target_dir)
...     source.copy(target)

# this will always return PureWindowsPath(), because PureWindowsPath('.') has no parts to replace
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('.')
>>> p.replace_parts('.', 'test')
PureWindowsPath()

# looks unexpected but is correct, since PureWindowsPath('/uploads') is a relative path in Windows
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> p.replace_parts('C:/downloads', '/uploads')
PureWindowsPath('uploads/pathlib.tar.gz')

# take care when replace, it might match on parts You are not aware of
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('c:/downloads/Downloads.tar.gz')
>>> p.replace_parts('downloads', 'uploads')
PureWindowsPath('c:/uploads/uploads.tar.gz')    # that was not intended !

# better
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('c:/downloads/Downloads.tar.gz')
>>> p.replace_parts('downloads', 'uploads', 1)
PureWindowsPath('c:/uploads/Downloads.tar.gz')

# much better
>>> p = PureWindowsPath('c:/downloads/Downloads.tar.gz')
>>> p.replace_parts('c:/downloads', 'c:/uploads')
PureWindowsPath('c:/uploads/Downloads.tar.gz')
```

# shutil wrappers

Path.copy(target, follow_symlinks)

: wraps shutil.copy, see: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html>

```python
>>> import pathlib3x as pathlib
>>> s = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> t = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz.backup')
>>> s.copy(t)
```

Path.copy2(target, follow_symlinks=True)

: wraps shutil.copy2, see: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html>

```python
>>> import pathlib3x as pathlib
>>> s = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> t = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz.backup')
>>> s.copy2(t)
```

Path.copyfile(target, follow_symlinks)

: wraps shutil.copyfile, see: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html>

```python
>>> import pathlib3x as pathlib
>>> s = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> t = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz.backup')
>>> s.copyfile(t)
```

Path.copymode(target, follow_symlinks=True)

: wraps shutil.copymode, see: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html>

```python
>>> import pathlib3x as pathlib
>>> s = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> t = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz.backup')
>>> s.copymode(t)
```

Path.copystat(target, follow_symlinks=True)

: wraps shutil.copystat, see: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html>

```python
>>> import pathlib3x as pathlib
>>> s = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz')
>>> t = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/pathlib.tar.gz.backup')
>>> s.copystat(t)
```

Path.copytree(target, symlinks=False, ignore=None, copy_function=copy2, ignore_dangling_symlinks=True, dirs_exists_ok=False)

: wraps shutil.copytree, see: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html>

  dirs_exists_ok=True will raise a TypeError on Python Versions \< 3.8

```python
>>> import pathlib3x as pathlib
>>> s = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads')
>>> t = pathlib.Path('c:/temp/Backups')
>>> s.copytree(t)
```

Path.rmtree(ignore_errors=False, onerror=None)

: wraps shutil.rmtree, see: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html>

```python
>>> import pathlib3x as pathlib
>>> p = pathlib.Path('c:/Downloads/old')
>>> p.rmtree()
```

# Caveats of pathlib3x

```python
>>> import pathlib3x
>>> import pathlib

>>> pathlib3x_path = pathlib3x.Path('some_path')  # this might happen in another module !
>>> pathlib_path = pathlib.Path('some_path')
>>> isinstance(pathlib3x_path, pathlib.Path)
False
>>> isinstance(pathlib_path, pathlib3x.Path)
False

# in such cases were You need to mix pathlib and pathlib3x in different modules, use:
>>> pathlib3x_path.Path.is_path_instance(pathlib3x_path)
True
>>> pathlib3x_path.Path.is_path_instance(pathlib_path)
True
```

So dont mix pathlib with pathlib3x and expect that objects are an instance of Pathlib and vice versa.
This can happen easily if You have many Modules. Just keep it in mind !

## Usage from Commandline

```
Usage: pathlib3x [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

  backport of pathlib 3.10 to python 3.6, 3.7, 3.8, 3.9 with a few extensions

Options:
  --version                     Show the version and exit.
  --traceback / --no-traceback  return traceback information on cli
  -h, --help                    Show this message and exit.

Commands:
  info  get program informations
```

## Installation and Upgrade

- Before You start, its highly recommended to update pip and setup tools:

```
python -m pip --upgrade pip
python -m pip --upgrade setuptools
```

- to install the latest release from PyPi via pip (recommended):

```
python -m pip install --upgrade pathlib3x
```

- to install the latest release from PyPi via pip, including test dependencies:

```
python -m pip install --upgrade pathlib3x[test]
```

- to install the latest version from github via pip:

```
python -m pip install --upgrade git+https://github.com/bitranox/pathlib3x.git
```

- include it into Your requirements.txt:

```
# Insert following line in Your requirements.txt:
# for the latest Release on pypi:
pathlib3x

# for the latest development version :
pathlib3x @ git+https://github.com/bitranox/pathlib3x.git

# to install and upgrade all modules mentioned in requirements.txt:
python -m pip install --upgrade -r /<path>/requirements.txt
```

- to install the latest development version, including test dependencies from source code:

```
# cd ~
$ git clone https://github.com/bitranox/pathlib3x.git
$ cd pathlib3x
python -m pip install -e .[test]
```

- via makefile:
  makefiles are a very convenient way to install. Here we can do much more,
  like installing virtual environments, clean caches and so on.

```shell
# from Your shell's homedirectory:
$ git clone https://github.com/bitranox/pathlib3x.git
$ cd pathlib3x

# to run the tests:
$ make test

# to install the package
$ make install

# to clean the package
$ make clean

# uninstall the package
$ make uninstall
```

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x._Flavour

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x._Flavour.join_parsed_parts
documentation:
Join the two paths represented by the respective
(drive, root, parts) tuples.  Return a new (drive, root, parts) tuple.

A flavour implements a particular (platform-specific) set of path
semantics.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x._WindowsFlavour.splitroot
documentation: Interesting findings about extended paths:
* '\\?\c:\a' is an extended path, which bypasses normal Windows API
path processing. Thus relative paths are not resolved and slash is not
translated to backslash. It has the native NT path limit of 32767
characters, but a bit less after resolving device symbolic links,
such as '\??\C:' => '\Device\HarddiskVolume2'.
* '\\?\c:/a' looks for a device named 'C:/a' because slash is a
regular name character in the object namespace.
* '\\?\c:\foo/bar' is invalid because '/' is illegal in NT filesystems.
The only path separator at the filesystem level is backslash.
* '//?/c:\a' and '//?/c:/a' are effectively equivalent to '\\.\c:\a' and
thus limited to MAX_PATH.
* Prior to Windows 8, ANSI API bytes paths are limited to MAX_PATH,
even with the '\\?\' prefix.

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x._Selector

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x._Selector.select_from
documentation:
Iterate over all child paths of `parent_path` matched by this
selector.  This can contain parent_path itself.

A selector matches a specific glob pattern part against the children
of a given path.

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x._PathParents
documentation:
This object provides sequence-like access to the logical ancestors
of a path.  Don't try to construct it yourself.
Public API

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.__new__
documentation:
Construct a PurePath from one or several strings and or existing
PurePath objects.  The strings and path objects are combined so as
to yield a canonicalized path, which is incorporated into the
new PurePath object.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.__str__
documentation:
Return the string representation of the path, suitable for
passing to system calls.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.as_posix
documentation:
Return the string representation of the path with forward (/)
slashes.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.__bytes__
documentation:
Return the bytes representation of the path.  This is only
recommended to use under Unix.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.as_uri
documentation: Return the path as a 'file' URI.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.anchor
documentation: The concatenation of the drive and root, or ''.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.name
documentation: The final path component, if any.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.suffix
documentation:
The final component's last suffix, if any.

This includes the leading period. For example: '.txt'

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.suffixes
documentation:
A list of the final component's suffixes, if any.

These include the leading periods. For example: ['.tar', '.gz']

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.stem
documentation: The final path component, minus its last suffix.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.with_name
documentation: Return a new path with the file name changed.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.with_stem
documentation: Return a new path with the stem changed.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.with_suffix
documentation:
Return a new path with the file suffix changed.  If the path
has no suffix, add given suffix.  If the given suffix is an empty
string, remove the suffix from the path.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.relative_to
documentation:
Return the relative path to another path identified by the passed
arguments.  If the operation is not possible (because this is not
a subpath of the other path), raise ValueError.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.is_relative_to
documentation: Return True if the path is relative to another path or False.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.parts
documentation:
An object providing sequence-like access to the
components in the filesystem path.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.joinpath
documentation:
Combine this path with one or several arguments, and return a
new path representing either a subpath (if all arguments are relative
paths) or a totally different path (if one of the arguments is
anchored).

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.parent
documentation: The logical parent of the path.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.parents
documentation: A sequence of this path's logical parents.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.is_absolute
documentation:
True if the path is absolute (has both a root and, if applicable,
a drive).

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.is_reserved
documentation:
Return True if the path contains one of the special names reserved

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.match

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.append_suffix
documentation:
Non-Standard Pathlib Method, added by bitranox. The suffix has to start with '.'
Return a new path with the file suffix appended.
If the given suffix is an empty string, do not append anything
PurePath non-standard methods added by bitranox

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.replace_parts
documentation:
Non-Standard Pathlib Method, added by bitranox.
this replaces the parts of a path as You would expect from str.replace(old, new, count)
It's useful to calculate the target path for file copy operations.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePath.is_path_instance
documentation: returns True when __obj is an Instance of pathlib or pathlib3x PurePath

Base class for manipulating paths without I/O.

PurePath represents a filesystem path and offers operations which
don't imply any actual filesystem I/O.  Depending on your system,
instantiating a PurePath will return either a PurePosixPath or a
PureWindowsPath object.  You can also instantiate either of these classes
directly, regardless of your system.

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PurePosixPath
documentation:
PurePath subclass for non-Windows systems.

On a POSIX system, instantiating a PurePath should return this object.
However, you can also instantiate it directly on any system.

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PureWindowsPath
documentation:
PurePath subclass for Windows systems.

On a Windows system, instantiating a PurePath should return this object.
However, you can also instantiate it directly on any system.

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.cwd
documentation:
Return a new path pointing to the current working directory
(as returned by os.getcwd()).
Public API

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.home
documentation:
Return a new path pointing to the user's home directory (as
returned by os.path.expanduser('~')).

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.samefile
documentation:
Return whether other_path is the same or not as this file
(as returned by os.path.samefile()).

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.iterdir
documentation:
Iterate over the files in this directory.  Does not yield any
result for the special paths '.' and '..'.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.glob
documentation:
Iterate over this subtree and yield all existing files (of any
kind, including directories) matching the given relative pattern.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.rglob
documentation:
Recursively yield all existing files (of any kind, including
directories) matching the given relative pattern, anywhere in
this subtree.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.absolute
documentation:
Return an absolute version of this path by prepending the current
working directory. No normalization or symlink resolution is performed.

Use resolve() to get the canonical path to a file.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.resolve
documentation:
Make the path absolute, resolving all symlinks on the way and also
normalizing it.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.stat
documentation:
Return the result of the stat() system call on this path, like
os.stat() does.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.owner
documentation: Return the login name of the file owner.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.group
documentation: Return the group name of the file gid.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.open
documentation:
Open the file pointed by this path and return a file object, as
the built-in open() function does.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.read_bytes
documentation: Open the file in bytes mode, read it, and close the file.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.read_text
documentation: Open the file in text mode, read it, and close the file.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.write_bytes
documentation: Open the file in bytes mode, write to it, and close the file.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.write_text
documentation: Open the file in text mode, write to it, and close the file.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.readlink
documentation: Return the path to which the symbolic link points.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.touch
documentation: Create this file with the given access mode, if it doesn't exist.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.mkdir
documentation: Create a new directory at this given path.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.chmod
documentation: Change the permissions of the path, like os.chmod().

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.lchmod
documentation:
Like chmod(), except if the path points to a symlink, the symlink's
permissions are changed, rather than its target's.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.unlink
documentation:
Remove this file or link.
If the path is a directory, use rmdir() instead.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.rmdir
documentation: Remove this directory.  The directory must be empty.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.lstat
documentation:
Like stat(), except if the path points to a symlink, the symlink's
status information is returned, rather than its target's.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.rename
documentation:
Rename this path to the target path.

The target path may be absolute or relative. Relative paths are
interpreted relative to the current working directory, *not* the
directory of the Path object.

Returns the new Path instance pointing to the target path.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.replace
documentation:
Rename this path to the target path, overwriting if that path exists.

The target path may be absolute or relative. Relative paths are
interpreted relative to the current working directory, *not* the
directory of the Path object.

Returns the new Path instance pointing to the target path.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.symlink_to
documentation:
Make this path a symlink pointing to the target path.
Note the order of arguments (link, target) is the reverse of os.symlink.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.hardlink_to
documentation:
Make this path a hard link pointing to the same file as *target*.

Note the order of arguments (self, target) is the reverse of os.link's.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.exists
documentation: Whether this path exists.
Convenience functions for querying the stat results

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_dir
documentation: Whether this path is a directory.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_file
documentation:
Whether this path is a regular file (also True for symlinks pointing
to regular files).

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_mount
documentation: Check if this path is a POSIX mount point

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_symlink
documentation: Whether this path is a symbolic link.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_block_device
documentation: Whether this path is a block device.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_char_device
documentation: Whether this path is a character device.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_fifo
documentation: Whether this path is a FIFO.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.is_socket
documentation: Whether this path is a socket.

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.expanduser
documentation:
Return a new path with expanded ~ and ~user constructs
(as returned by os.path.expanduser)

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.copy
documentation: wraps shutil.copy
Path non-standard methods added by bitranox

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.copy2
documentation: wraps shutil.copy2

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.copyfile
documentation: wraps shutil.copyfile

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.copymode
documentation: wraps shutil.copymode

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.copystat
documentation: wraps shutil.copystat

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.copytree
documentation: wraps shutil.copytree - dirs_exists_ok=True only allowed on python >= 3.8

methods.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.Path.rmtree
documentation: wraps shutil.rmtree)

PurePath subclass that can make system calls.

Path represents a filesystem path but unlike PurePath, also offers
methods to do system calls on path objects. Depending on your system,
instantiating a Path will return either a PosixPath or a WindowsPath
object. You can also instantiate a PosixPath or WindowsPath directly,
but cannot instantiate a WindowsPath on a POSIX system or vice versa.
Filesystem-accessing classes

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.PosixPath
documentation:
Path subclass for non-Windows systems.

On a POSIX system, instantiating a Path should return this object.

classes.name: pathlib3x.pathlib3x.WindowsPath
documentation:
Path subclass for Windows systems.

On a Windows system, instantiating a Path should return this object.
